entry.title: Re: How to update sequence number /SCWM/STOP_SEQ in heade...
entry.content:
SAP has offered to update stop sequence / loading sequence manually through warehouse monitor.

For ODO, it will be stop sequence. In case we have one truck with multiple deliveries to multiple customers, we can define the stop sequence to the delivery loaded in reversal sequence).

For ODO item level, it will be loading sequence. e.g in one truck, some items might be fragile, some sturdy, some might need to unload first. In this case, you can define ODO item level loading sequence. 

However I haven't figured out how this will work with the loading WO/WTs. You may be able to debug and find out more. The methods are:

ODO - update stop sequence - /SCWM/WHRHEAD_OUT_UPD_LOAD_SEQ

ODO item - update load sequence - /SCWM/WHRITEM_OUT_UPD_LOAD_SEQ

Hi all,

I have a question on performance management forms creation. Suppose, there is an employee in the organization, who is not related to the employee whose form is created. In the form, the roles which are available is manager, A-manager, second-level manager, HR etc. But this employee is not related. 2. Employee central is not activated

🎃 Spooky memories of SAP Web IDE 🎃

Back in the day, when everything moved slower than a spider spinning its web, SAP Web IDE was the tool we depended on—waiting for that loading screen to finish felt like an eternity!

entry.title: Re: html code for email body
entry.content:
Here is the CL_BCS_CONVERT=>STRING_TO_SOLI method. there is no character limit.

DATA: lv_html       TYPE string.
DATA: gw_text_bcsy TYPE soli_tab.

lv_html = '<html><body> html body or table data'.
lv_html = lv_html && '<table border="1">'.
lv_html = lv_html && '<TR><TH>Company</TH><TH>column label1.</TH><TH>column label2</TH></TR>'.

LOOP AT it_table INTO wa_area.
lv_html  = lv_html && '<tr><td>' && wa_area-xxx && '</td><td>' && wa_area-xxx && '</td></tr>'
endloop.

lv_html  = lv_html &&'</TABLE></body><html>'.
 CALL METHOD cl_bcs_convert=>string_to_soli
            EXPORTING
              iv_string = lv_html
            RECEIVING
              et_soli   = gw_text_bcsy.
          CLEAR : lv_html.


Regards,
Dharmesh Thanki

entry.content:
Hi Soma,

you can use the AdaptUI to flexibly configure the UI layout of the screen.

While in display mode, you can add or remove fields as per your requirements:

While using the AdaptUI in the edit mode however, it is currently not possible to remove existing fields, rather one can only add additional fields to a new section.

Providing some snippets of the code:

FETCHING DATA FROM LIPS table

 SELECT deliverydocument,                      "vbeln
                     deliverydocumentitem,                  "posnr
                     material,                              "matnr

                     actualdeliveryquantity,                "lfimg

                     deliveryquantityunit,                  "vrkme

entry.title: Breathing New Life into SAP Brownfield+: 5 Challenges and 5 Steps with BTP, UX, and Innovation Trife
entry.content:
Background

Brownfield projects+, while offering a tempting shortcut to expansion, come with their own set of hurdles. Despite the acknowledged limitations of brownfield+ upgrades compared to greenfield projects, many customers are drawn to this approach due to the significant risks and costs associated with greenfield implementations.

The Challenge and The Step

1. Integrated Legacy Systems: The Weight of the Past

Outdated integrated systems is a common obstacle in brownfield projects. Integrating new systems with legacy infrastructure can lead to compatibility issues, data silos, and security vulnerabilities and may also need clients to rewire the integrations to the new system.

Consolidation of Integrations: . We should begin by ranking each integration based on its business criticality and the potential positive business impact of modernization. We still need to rewire all the old integrations to the new SAP Brownfield + systems and test them to ensure that data is flowing through correctly and processes are working as expected. This may be right time for us to assess whether rewiring or upgrading middle-ware or redesigning interfaces using new BTP technologies is the right thing to do based on risk vs reward vs cost.

 

Conquer Integration Chaos

1. Minimize Integration Debt: Identify and eliminate unnecessary integrations, focusing on those that deliver clear business value.

2. Prioritize Integration Journeys: Define and prioritize key integration journeys that directly impact business efficiencies and outcomes.

3. Establish Integration Standards:Implement clear standards and best practices for future integrations to ensure consistency, maintainability, and scalability.

2. Data Inconsistencies: Unearthing Hidden Treasures

Brownfield+ projects often inherit messy data landscapes. Inconsistent formats, duplicate entries, and poor data quality hinder decision-making and operational efficiency.

Unearthing Hidden Treasures : Now is the ideal time to uncover valuable data assets and establish robust data governance. We should identify business processes where analytic insights and improving poor data quality can yield significant business benefits. Then, we can define and implement appropriate data policies and governance mechanisms to ensure data accuracy, consistency, and reliability in these areas.

 

3 data gold mine tips, link process and data and analytics KPI's together !
  1. Fill Data Holes : Leverage process mining tools to identify data holes that are causing process inefficiencies
  2. Magnify : Assess and rank both business processes and data objects based on their potential business value and impact on desired outcomes.
  3. Build Process and Data Telescope : Prioritize process analytic insights and attributes that helps to see your business and processes in different dimensions and re-think analytic journey and get data quality right.

3. Spice up the Brownfield+ User Adoption: Use the UX as a cornerstone

Don't let UX become an afterthought in brownfield+ projects, this can be crucial factor to sell your S4 HANA brownfield + business case to business! Prioritize customer and business user experience by identifying key customer and business persona journeys that would benefit from a refresh. Leverage the power of generative AI, Web4, and 5G technologies to seamlessly embed modern and engaging UX elements into these end to end journeys.

 

Don't think SAP, think journeys end to end to identify areas of highest business impact!
  1. Engage UX and Business Experts : Conduct a poll to identify customer and business journeys that can be improved
  2. Heart the high impact UX journeys : Map user journeys to business KPI's and get businesses to heart/like the UX journeys that will be be very useful to business
  3. Magic Business UX Value Formulae : Use Gen AI to map UX journeys to value case and develop UX Journeys cost vs value case

4. Grease the Wheels and Prune the Code

While achieving a pristine core codebase might seem like a distant dream in brownfield+ projects with extensive legacy code, remember that even seemingly insurmountable challenges can be overcome with bold vision and determined action. If Elon Musk can revolutionize the automotive and aerospace industries, surely we can find innovative ways to streamline SAP codebase. Let's not be daunted by the scale of the task, but instead, approach it with the same spirit of innovation and ambition that drives groundbreaking achievements.

 

May not be fully clean but easier to scale in future
  1. Trimming the Fat : Time for some code spring cleaning! Let's hunt down those unused custom objects and code, then hit delete and free up some space in our system
  2. Polish Your Custom Crown Jewels : Let's sharpen our competitive edge! We'll leverage cutting-edge BTP,AI and cloud technologies to revamp and supercharge the custom objects that set us apart in the market.
  3. No More Code Clutter: Time for a Fresh Future: Let's create a sunset plan for currently, moderately used, outdated code. We'll map out how to redesign or retire this existing code, set clear expiration dates, and establish guidelines to ensure all new custom code is future-proof and aligned with our long-term goals.

5. Create a Landscape That Bends, Not Breaks

ERP and legacy modernization projects often become overly complex and costly due to a perfect storm of challenges: inadequate documentation, lack of automated testing for continuous improvement, a poor understanding of existing data and processes, and insufficient availability of crucial business resources. Brownfield+ projects offer a prime opportunity to **bleep** these recurring project nightmares in the bud.

 

DevOps: The Conductor of Operational Symphony
  1. Instant Process Mapping where Gen AI Meets Signavio : Leverage new Gen AI and Signavio tools to augment your process flows and generate documentations for critical end to end process scenarios.
  2. Shift Gears for Continuous Automated Testing : Don't let those end-to-end brownfield test scripts gather dust! Automate them, document them, and turn them into a valuable asset for future testing and maintenance of your brownfield project.
  3. The Change Express for delivering value non-stop : Reuse change management training and enable continuous release mechanisms where possible to deploy applications quicker where possible using new GIT frameworks. Let's inject some fun into change management! Imagine a Gen AI bot as your guide, helping you create engaging podcasts, interactive diagrams, and other creative content to communicate process changes in a fun and memorable way.

By embracing a strategic blend of BTP, UX design, and a forward-thinking mindset, we can transform brownfield projects into catalysts for innovation and growth.

Now go forth and conquer those brownfield challenges! We have the power to transform brownfield limitations into strategic advantages.

Don't shy away from bold goals. Even seemingly daunting legacy systems can be modernized and optimized for a brighter future.
Prioritize user experience. A seamless and intuitive UX can make all the difference in driving user adoption and maximizing the value of your brownfield investment.
Embrace automation and continuous improvement. From code optimization to testing and deployment, automation is key to unlocking efficiency and agility.
Make it fun! Change management doesn't have to be dry and tedious. Inject creativity and engagement into the process to inspire your team and stakeholders.

entry.title: Re: Settlement Periods for Service Contracts
entry.content:
Hi Soma,

what is the exact reasoning behind wanting a settlement period starting on the 16th? This wouldn't be functionally correct in my opinion, as you would be ignoring the first 15 days (01.11.24 - 15.11.24) of the contract's. Furthermore, the final settlement period would then be 16.10.25 - 15.11.25, which would then also be exceeding the contract end date (31.10.25).

I'm not aware of the exact business requirements in your case, however a simpler approach would be to have monthly settlement periods that are aligned with the contract start and end dates, and to use a billing date with an offset of 14 days:

Best regards
Varun

entry.title: Re: BusinessObjects - Trending DB Configuration for Oracle
entry.content:
I have this kind of setup, and I remember it was a little tricky.

First, i use one common schema for Auditing, Monitoring and BI Commentary. That is so far the best option when using Oracle. According to the KBAs i have setup the Auding DB Config in the way you describe, and in Monitoring "Use Auditing Database".

Additionally, you need to get the JDBC drivers from Oracle and put them in a Oracle Folder, like D:\Oracle in case of Windows. Then you also need to adapt the file oracle.sbo in the folder [BO Folder]\dataAccess\connectionServer\jdbc\ and check this entry:

<DataBase Active="Yes" Name="Oracle 12">  <!-- This setting is necessary for Monitoring! -->  
  <Aliases>  
    <Alias>Oracle</Alias>  
  </Aliases>  
  <JDBCDriver>  <!-- Uncomment and edit the following lines to define java classes required by JDBC driver -->  
    <ClassPath>  
      <Path>D:\oracle\ojdbc6.jar</Path>  <!-- Path the the JDBC driver mentioned before  -->
    </ClassPath>  
    <Parameter Name="JDBC Class">oracle.jdbc.OracleDriver</Parameter>  
    <Parameter Name="URL Format">jdbc:oracle:thin:@//$DATASOURCE$/$DATABASE$</Parameter>  
  </JDBCDriver>  
  <Parameter Name="Extensions">oracle12,oracle,jdbc</Parameter>  
  <Parameter Name="Escape Character">/</Parameter>  
  <Parameter Name="Force Execute">Always</Parameter>  
  <Parameter Name="Catalog Separator">.</Parameter>  
  <Parameter Name="Driver Capabilities">Query,Procedures,Cancel</Parameter> 
</DataBase>

Note, that i have a setup for Oracle12 using the ojdbc6.jar even though we run Oracle 19. I think to remember that this was the only option that worked, using newer JDBC drivers and the approriate SBO entry did not work out. As this is just basic INSERTs that BO is doing, this is completely fine. Maybe in the current SP4 that has changed and the newer drivers work as well, but for our environment that was the only configuration that worked well.

HTH a little 

regards, bernhard

entry.title: Extract ANLZ Time-Dependent Asset Allocations Data in CSV File Using ABAP Program For Selected Cost
entry.content:
FIXED ASSET - Extract ANLZ Time-Dependent Asset Allocations Data in CSV File Using ABAP Program For Selected Cost Center

ABAP Program - Z_ANLZ_FIXEDASSET 

  • Program Type – ABAP 
  • ABAP Program Name – Z_ANLZ_FIXEDASSET 
  • Tcode - ZHM_ANLZ_A 
  • Output File Location - “C:\tmp” 
  • Output File Name- ANLZ.CSV 
  • Output File Type – CSV (Column with Semicolon separator (;) 
  • SAP Table Name – ANLZ, ANLA 
  • List of fields to be extracted -  
  • ANLN1 
  • KOSTL 

------ABAP Program Start From Here--------------------

REPORT  Z_ANLZ_FIXEDASSET.
 
TYPE-POOLS: VRM.
 
DATA: NAME  TYPE VRM_ID,
      LIST  TYPE VRM_VALUES,
      VALUE LIKE LINE OF LIST.
 
 
PARAMETERS: ENTITY(10) AS LISTBOX VISIBLE LENGTH 10.
PARAMETERS : OPATH TYPE STRING DEFAULT '\\MyServer\tmp\ANLZ.csv'.
AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT.
 
NAME = 'ENTITY'.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK01'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK01'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK75'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK75'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK21'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK21'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK31'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK31'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK41'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK41'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK02'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK02'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK73'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK73'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK04'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK04'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
VALUE-KEY = 'UK61'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK61'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.
 
CALL FUNCTION 'VRM_SET_VALUES' EXPORTING ID = NAME VALUES = LIST.
 
START-OF-SELECTION.
 
type-pools:TRUXS.
data: begin of itab occurs 0,
      anln1 like anlz-anln1,
  kostl like anlz-kostl,
   BUKRS like anlz-BUKRS,
      end of itab.
data:  itab1 type TRUXS_T_TEXT_DATA.
 select  ANLZ~anln1
ANLZ~kostl
   ANLZ~BUKRS
         from ANLZ INNER JOIN ANLA ON ANLZ~ANLN1 = ANLA~ANLN1
             into table itab
WHERE ANLA~DEAKT ='00000000' AND ANLZ~BUKRS = ENTITY.
 
CALL FUNCTION 'SAP_CONVERT_TO_CSV_FORMAT'
 EXPORTING
   I_FIELD_SEPERATOR          = ','
  TABLES
    I_TAB_SAP_DATA             = itab
 CHANGING
   I_TAB_CONVERTED_DATA       =  itab1
 EXCEPTIONS
   CONVERSION_FAILED          = 1
   OTHERS                     = 2
          .
IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
 MESSAGE ID SY-MSGID TYPE SY-MSGTY NUMBER SY-MSGNO
         WITH SY-MSGV1 SY-MSGV2 SY-MSGV3 SY-MSGV4.
ENDIF.
 
   CALL FUNCTION 'GUI_DOWNLOAD'
    EXPORTING
      filename = OPATH
    TABLES
      data_tab = itab1
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS   = 1.

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: Devtoberfest, New Features, Popular Content, and More
entry.content:
Lest you think that SAP Community Voice updates have been silenced, we’re back to share the latest site updates, spotlight popular posts, and promote cool new areas for you to explore!

 

Featured Content

 

Still Time to Participate in Devtoberfest 2024!

Devtoberfest wraps up on October 25, so you still have time to participate in this event. If you’re interested in coding and collaborating – and earning badges (and maybe even prizes) for your efforts – you’ll want to join the Devtoberfest group, learn about the event-related activities, and join in!

Visit the group

 

Contest: My SAP TechEd Virtual Viewing Setup

Also ending on October 25: the “My SAP TechEd Virtual viewing setup” contest. SAP TechEd may be over, but you can still show off how you viewed the virtual event – and vote on contestants. Head on over to the SAP TechEd group to get contest details, share your entry, and view (and vote for) submissions.

Learn about the contest

 

Is Data Warehouse Required with SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics?

If that question grabbed your attention, you’ll want to read this blog post and check out the comments. The author (a consultant) goes through several use cases before sharing his closing remarks (and inviting feedback – of which there is plenty). See if you agree and join the conversation!

Read the blog post

 

Highlights from 2H 2024 Release of Performance and Goals Management

One of our members takes a closer look at the latest set of enhancements available in the 2H 2024 release of the SAP SuccessFactors platform. This post covers topics such as AI-assisted capabilities, improvements to goal management and continuous performance management, and delegation for performance review.

Read the blog post

 

Community News

 

Check Out the Latest SAP Community Content

Since our last SAP Community Voice newsletter, we added several new location groups: Charlotte, Madrid, and Pune. We also launched an interest group for Procurement Professionals in LAC and introduced a new discussion board for the Putting AI Ethics into Practice at SAP course. In addition, we published a new topic page dedicated to SAP Digital Currency Hub. Check out these new areas and make sure to join the groups that interest you!

 

The Latest Improvements to the SAP Community Platform

You may have already noticed these updates to the platform, but just in case…

You can now sort discussions, blog posts, and knowledge-base boards by “creation date.”

View counts have been added to blog tiles, and the date format has been changed to yyyy MMM dd. (Don’t like that format? You can switch to a style you prefer via My settings > Preferences > Display. Once there, you can modify the “Date display format” field. Just make sure to use uppercase M for the month.)

We’re expecting to roll out more improvements in a few weeks, so look for more updates later this month or in early November.

entry.title: The RFP Revolution: From Box-Ticking to Perfect-Fit SAP Partnerships
entry.content:
This year, I've witnessed a fascinating contrast in the world of SAP RFPs. I've had the opportunity to be involved in both ends of the spectrum: from exciting, purpose-driven proposals that prioritized value and innovation to more traditional, checklist-based approaches.

It's been inspiring to see clients championing this shift towards a more collaborative and value-focused selection process for their SAP implementation partners. I'm eager to share my observations and insights on how this transformation is unfolding and what it means for the future of successful SAP projects. I'm also keen to hear about your own experiences and perspectives on this evolving landscape.

1. From Requirements List to Interactive Blueprint:

Traditional: A lengthy document outlining every technical specification, often stifling creativity and limiting the exploration of innovative solutions.

Modern: Host a collaborative workshop where client and potential partners jointly develop a visual blueprint of the desired future state. This interactive approach encourages open dialogue, challenges assumptions, and fosters a shared understanding of the vision.

2. From Price Comparisons to Value-Based Assessments:

Traditional: A focus on the lowest bid, often leading to compromises on quality, experience, and long-term value.

Modern: Request a "Value Proposition Canvas" from each potential partner. This canvas outlines the partner's understanding of your key business pains, their proposed solutions, and the quantifiable value they aim to deliver. This shifts the focus from cost to return on investment.

3. From One-Size-Fits-All to Tailored Proof of Concepts:

Traditional: Generic presentations and case studies that may not resonate with your specific challenges.

Modern: Challenge potential partners to develop a tailored Proof of Concept (POC) addressing a critical business problem you face. This provides tangible evidence of their expertise, problem-solving approach, and ability to deliver tangible results.

4. From Formal Interviews to "Day in the Life" Simulations:

Traditional: Structured interviews that may not reveal true team dynamics and cultural fit.

Modern: Conduct a "Day in the Life" workshop where your team and the potential partner collaborate on a simulated project scenario. This provides valuable insights into communication styles, problem-solving approaches, and overall team synergy.

5. From Static Documents to Dynamic Collaboration Platforms:

Traditional: Paper-based or static digital RFPs that limit interaction and feedback.

Revolutionized: Utilize collaborative platforms that allow for real-time communication by asking intelligent questions, document sharing, and feedback loops throughout the RFP process. This fosters transparency, encourages engagement, and ensures everyone is aligned.

The RFP Revolution: A Call to Action

By embracing these concrete examples and adopting a more collaborative, value-driven, and holistic approach, we can transform the RFP process from boring check list document into a catalyst for forging truly trans-formative SAP partnerships. Let's modernize the procurement landscape together!

Here's how we can reshape the traditional RFP's commercial strategy, shifting from a cost-centric approach to one that fuels innovation:

Traditional RFP (Cost Focus) ➡️ Revolutionized RFP (Innovation Focus)

Price-Driven Selection ➡️ Value-Based Assessment:

Old: Lowest bidder often wins, potentially sacrificing quality and long-term value.

New: Evaluate proposals on Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), ROI projections, and the partner's commitment to continuous improvement and value delivery.

Fixed Scope & Requirements ➡️ Agile & Outcome-Oriented:

Old: Rigidly defined scope limits flexibility for innovative solutions to emerge during the project.

New: Define desired business outcomes and allow for iterative development, incorporating new technologies or approaches as needed.

Payment Tied to Deliverables ➡️Shared Risk & Reward Models:

Old: Vendor gets paid for completing tasks, even if the overall solution doesn't meet expectations.

New: Explore gain-sharing agreements, outcome-based pricing, or joint innovation funds to incentivize partners to deliver exceptional results.

Key Questions to Ask Potential Partners (Innovation Focus):

  1. How will you leverage emerging technologies (e.g., AI, automation) to drive innovation and efficiency in our SAP implementation? Give an example of your business problem to solve!

  2. Can you provide examples of how you've helped other clients in our industry achieve breakthrough results through innovative SAP solutions?

  3. How will you ensure that our SAP implementation remains flexible and adaptable to future business changes and technological advancements?Give an example of your business problem to solve!

  4. What is your approach to knowledge transfer and co-innovation, ensuring that our team is empowered to drive ongoing innovation with SAP? Give an example of your business problem to solve!

entry.title: WWI / why is is still the most troublesome tool? / 3E WWI Content => who use it as delivered?
entry.content:
Dear all

I have the feeling: people are not reading the SAP documentation, how to set up a WWI Server and how to connect this to SAP and to prepare all the needed jobs etc.

But this "frustration" I have to solve by my own.

Luckily: with Product Compliance we will see a different solution. As the solution is not final... we can only hope to get the same flexibility.

Just learning on the top the 3E Templates.

If I compare them with our own layout. .. to much complex; to many if/else/endif and use of the stack option

So in an nutshell: I would like to know: do you use 3E WWI layouts as "delivered" or are you changing a lot ? And if you change... in which chapter you change?

Still trying to understand 3E WWI Chapter 14; our current layout for chapter 14 is much more simpler

The Product Compliance solution does not need a "WWI Server (that is the good news); i hope that the performance will be good as well (for SDS; as Label laut/printing are anyhow not part of Product Compliance); 

C.B.

entry.title: HMAC authentication does not work
entry.content:
I need to talk to a server using REST with HMAC authentication. Before dealing with the server, I thought I would first write and validate the HMAC part and... that's exactly where I am stuck: the values I calculate in SAP are different from those I get using online calculators.

Here is my utility class:

CLASS zcl_auth_util DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CONSTANTS c_algo_sha256 TYPE string VALUE `SHA256`.

    CLASS-METHODS build_key
      IMPORTING if_password TYPE string
                if_salt     TYPE string
      EXPORTING ef_key      TYPE xstring.

    CLASS-METHODS build_hmac_response
      IMPORTING if_key        TYPE xstring
                if_iterations TYPE i
                if_data       TYPE string
      EXPORTING ef_result     TYPE string
                ef_resultx    TYPE xstring
                ef_resultb    TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.



CLASS ZCL_AUTH_UTIL IMPLEMENTATION.


* <SIGNATURE>---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
* | Static Public Method ZCL_AUTH_UTIL=>BUILD_HMAC_RESPONSE
* +-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
* | [--->] IF_KEY                         TYPE        XSTRING
* | [--->] IF_ITERATIONS                  TYPE        I
* | [--->] IF_DATA                        TYPE        STRING
* | [<---] EF_RESULT                      TYPE        STRING
* | [<---] EF_RESULTX                     TYPE        XSTRING
* | [<---] EF_RESULTB                     TYPE        STRING
* +--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------</SIGNATURE>
  METHOD build_hmac_response.
    DATA lv_hmacxstring   TYPE xstring.
    DATA lv_hmacb64string TYPE string.

    DATA(lv_data) = if_data.
    DO if_iterations TIMES.
      TRY.
          cl_abap_hmac=>calculate_hmac_for_char( EXPORTING if_algorithm     = c_algo_sha256
                                                           if_key           = if_key
                                                           if_data          = lv_data
                                                 IMPORTING ef_hmacstring    = lv_data
                                                           ef_hmacxstring   = lv_hmacxstring
                                                           ef_hmacb64string = lv_hmacb64string ).
        CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(e_txt).
          WRITE / e_txt->get_text( ).
      ENDTRY.
    ENDDO.
    ef_result = lv_data.
    ef_resultx = lv_hmacxstring.
    ef_resultb = lv_hmacb64string.
  ENDMETHOD.


* <SIGNATURE>---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
* | Static Public Method ZCL_AUTH_UTIL=>BUILD_KEY
* +-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
* | [--->] IF_PASSWORD                    TYPE        STRING
* | [--->] IF_SALT                        TYPE        STRING
* | [<---] EF_KEY                         TYPE        XSTRING
* +--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------</SIGNATURE>
  METHOD build_key.
    DATA(lv_keyx) = cl_abap_hmac=>string_to_xstring( |{ if_password }{ if_salt }| ).

    TRY.
        DATA(lr_digest) = cl_abap_message_digest=>get_instance( if_algorithm = c_algo_sha256 ).
        lr_digest->update( if_data = lv_keyx ).
        lr_digest->digest( ).
        ef_key = cl_abap_hmac=>string_to_xstring( lr_digest->to_string( ) ).
      CATCH cx_abap_message_digest.  "
    ENDTRY.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

And this is the test class:

*"* use this source file for your ABAP unit test classes
CLASS ltc_auth_util_test DEFINITION FOR TESTING RISK LEVEL HARMLESS DURATION SHORT.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS _01_key_hash         FOR TESTING.
    METHODS _02_hmac_1_iter_no_sha FOR TESTING.
    METHODS _03_hmac_1_iter_with_sha FOR TESTING.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS class_setup.
    CLASS-METHODS class_teardown.

    METHODS setup.
    METHODS teardown.

    CONSTANTS c_password TYPE string VALUE `this_is_a_test_password`.
    CONSTANTS c_salt TYPE string VALUE `N1cbsxNB2X1uR0i20YmE9iV9L5icQtXQQ5V+7quww24VK32IStgnMhp/BIcgIoFH7FTUDaYHyZvsVrLRZRH4Nw==`.
    CONSTANTS c_pwd_salt_sha256 TYPE string VALUE `55557945A262E5FB6C553CBA833D0F75A11D42B17EEF27C7288B2A6C8B7CB930`.
    CONSTANTS c_testdata TYPE string VALUE `this is a test message to be signed`.
    CONSTANTS c_hmac_of_pwd_salt_testdata TYPE string VALUE `5B78CD8ADA9CABC8A65AAF5B0AD8648DD021FF1C285BEA51A3DDD6CC2D9D7A07`.

    DATA mo_cut TYPE REF TO zcl_auth_util.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS ltc_auth_util_test IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD _01_key_hash.
    DATA l_exp TYPE xstring.
    DATA l_act TYPE xstring.

    TRY.
        l_exp = cl_abap_hmac=>string_to_xstring( if_input = c_pwd_salt_sha256 ).
      CATCH cx_abap_message_digest.  "
    ENDTRY.

    mo_cut->build_key(
      EXPORTING if_password = c_password
                if_salt     = c_salt
      IMPORTING ef_key      = l_act ).
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( exp = l_exp
                                        act = l_act
                                        msg = `Hashed key (pwd/salt) does not match expected value` ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD _02_hmac_1_iter_no_sha.
    DATA l_act_string TYPE string.

    TRY.
        DATA(lv_keyx) = cl_abap_hmac=>string_to_xstring(
* This is the correct SHA256 of the password/salt from the first test
*       if_input = c_pwd_salt_sha256
* but first we try with raw password+salt
        if_input = |{ c_password }{ c_salt }| ).

      CATCH cx_abap_message_digest.  "
    ENDTRY.

    mo_cut->build_hmac_response( EXPORTING if_key        = lv_keyx
                                           if_iterations = 1
                                           if_data       = c_testdata
                                 IMPORTING ef_result     = l_act_string ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_differs( exp = c_hmac_of_pwd_salt_testdata
                                        act = l_act_string
                                        msg = `HMAC with RAW password/salt matches hash, that's odd!` ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD _03_hmac_1_iter_with_sha.
    DATA l_act_string TYPE string.

    TRY.
        DATA(lv_keyx) = cl_abap_hmac=>string_to_xstring(
* This is the correct SHA256 of the password/salt from the first test
                            if_input = c_pwd_salt_sha256 ).
      CATCH cx_abap_message_digest.  "
    ENDTRY.

    mo_cut->build_hmac_response( EXPORTING if_key        = lv_keyx
                                           if_iterations = 1
                                           if_data       = c_testdata
                                 IMPORTING ef_result     = l_act_string ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( exp = c_hmac_of_pwd_salt_testdata
                                        act = l_act_string
                                        msg = 'HMAC with SHA256 of password/salt does not match expected hash' ).
  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD class_setup.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD class_teardown.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD setup.
    mo_cut = NEW #( ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD teardown.
    CLEAR mo_cut.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Test 1 and 2 pass, test 3 fails. Actually, test 2 was an afterthought: maybe the HMAC class is already doing a SHA256 on the key and I do not have to do it again? But no, apparently that is not the case and I am still clueless as to what might be the issue here.

Regardless of whether the remote server is expecting the key to be the SHA256 of password+salt or just of the password, it would be nice if my calculated values matched what the online calculators say 🙂

Any ideas? The code above is complete and runs ok in the latest Docker image for sure (but even at the office using a relatively ancient release, for that matter)

entry.title: How to print SAP B1 Invoice on the printer assigned in Report and Layout Manager using UI API
entry.content:
Hello,

I am trying to print the SAP B1 Marketing documents on the printer that is assigned in the Report and Layout Manager but it is always sent to the default printer using the SAP B1 SDK.

The code is as below:

 

// Connect to the printing services
CompanyService oCompanyService;
ReportLayoutsService oReportLayoutService;
ReportLayoutPrintParams oPrintParam;
oCompanyService = oCompany.GetCompanyService();
oReportLayoutService = oCompanyService.GetBusinessService(ServiceTypes.ReportLayoutsService);

// Setup the Report
ReportParams oReportParams = oReportLayoutService.GetDataInterface(ReportLayoutsServiceDataInterfaces.rlsdiReportParams);
oReportParams.ReportCode = "INV2";

// Get the default report layout
oPrintParam = oReportLayoutService.GetDataInterface(ReportLayoutsServiceDataInterfaces.rlsdiReportLayoutPrintParams);
oPrintParam.LayoutCode = oReportLayoutService.GetDefaultReport(oReportParams).LayoutCode;
oPrintParam.DocEntry = sapDocument.DocEntry;

oReportLayoutService.Print(oPrintParam);

 

Could you please let us know how we can print on the assigned printer.

Also, there can be another way of doing this by triggering the Print button (ActivateMenuItem) on the Toolbar, but I cannot seem to find the menu id for the same. 

Any help on this will be appreciated. Thank you.

@ANKIT_CHAUHAN

entry.title: Re: How to Create RAP Business Events in SAP S/4HANA On Premise 2022
entry.content:
To create RAP (Restful ABAP Programming) business events in SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2022:

  1. Set up your RAP environment and ensure the necessary services are active.
  2. Define the event model in your RAP business object.
  3. Use CDS views to capture and expose the relevant business data.
  4. Implement the event handling logic in ABAP, then trigger and publish the events.

Make sure to test thoroughly in your development environment before deployment.

entry.title: Avoid Overengineering in Software Development: Make it simple with KISS,DRY,YAGNI and MVP
entry.content:
Overengineering in software development is like building a Swiss Army knife when all you need is a simple screwdriver. It happens when developers create overly complex solutions for problems that could have been solved more simply. While the intentions behind overengineering might be good, such as aiming for flexibility, scalability, or robustness, it often leads to unnecessary complexity, increased costs, and slower development cycles.

I’m obviously a fan of clean code principles 🙈😅. In this blog, i will highlight the overengineering practices in SAP development and introduce you to practical strategies like DRY,KISS,YANGI, and maybe MVP that will help you avoid falling into the trap of over-engineering.

 

What Is Overengineering?

Overengineering occurs when developers build solutions that are far more complicated than required for the current problem. This could mean adding extra features, implementing complex architecture, or overgeneralizing code to cater to future needs that may never arise.

some examples from our SAP life :

  • Building overly complex custom ABAP modules when SAP S/4HANA already provides standard functionality to meet the requirement.
  • Implementing complex logic within SAP Fiori/freestyle UI5 apps when a simple Fiori Elements standard list report/object page App could deliver the required business value.

While it may seem prudent to "future-proof" the solution, this can backfire, leading to inflated development and maintenance efforts 😅.

The Hidden Cost of Overengineering

SAP implementations are often complex due to the nature of the enterprise environments they serve. SAP landscapes typically include a mix of complex integrated platforms, cloud services, and more. In such environments, developers are often tempted to create highly flexible and future-proof systems to meet all possible requirements. While this approach may seem like a safeguard against future changes, it can quickly lead to various tangible and intangible costs:

  • Increased Complexity: Complex solutions require more time to develop, test, and maintain, which can lead to budget overruns and timeline delays.
  • Reduced Agility: When a system is overly complex, it becomes harder to make changes quickly in response to new business requirements.
  • Higher Maintenance Costs: Overengineered solutions often require more effort to maintain, especially when custom code replaces standard SAP functionality.
  • Frustrated Business Stakeholders: Users may become frustrated when they see delays and costs rising while key functionalities are being deprioritized in favor of future-proofing that may never be needed.

YAGNI: You Aren't Gonna Need It

The YAGNI principle, which originated from the Extreme Programming (XP) methodology, advises developers not to implement functionality until it is absolutely necessary. It encourages a lean and focused approach, where only the most immediate and critical features are built.

In SAP projects, YAGNI can help avoid the tendency to add unnecessary complexity:

  • Avoid speculative features: SAP developers, especially when working with custom ABAP or UI5 applications, often anticipate potential future requirements. However, implementing such features too early can lead to bloated codebases. For example, designing overly generic data models or complex business logic for hypothetical use cases can complicate future enhancements.
  • Simplify development: By focusing on what is needed now, SAP teams can deliver faster and with more agility. This is particularly important in the rapidly changing enterprise environment, where business needs are often volatile. Whether it's developing custom apps using SAP Fiori or creating custom integrations in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), YAGNI encourages the team to focus on real requirements, not assumptions.
  • Maintainability: With fewer unnecessary features, the SAP landscape remains easier to maintain and upgrade over time, reducing technical debt and improving the long-term ROI of the project.

MVP: Minimum Viable Product

MVP focuses on delivering the simplest version of a product that meets the core needs of users, allowing teams to validate their ideas quickly.

In my opinion, MVP approach is ideal for testing new ideas, especially when you have a concept you want to bring to market or when you’re operating within a budget and need a quick, straightforward solution

Example: Developing a  SAP Fiori application that is intended to manage employee requests for leave. Instead of building an extensive system with complex reporting, notifications, and user roles, the MVP could focus solely on the essential features: a simple user interface for submitting leave requests and a basic admin interface for approving them. This allows for quick feedback from users, enabling the team to prioritize additional features based on real needs rather than assumptions.

DRY: Don’t Repeat Yourself

The DRY principle in my opinion is the main approach to better code refactoring since it aligns with the clean code principles that encourage avoiding code duplication by abstracting and reusing code wherever possible. Popularized by the book, The Pragmatic Programmer, the DRY principle states that, “every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation within a system.” Using the principle, logic, or algorithms that have certain functionality should only appear once in an application. This also includes refraining from using the same solution or approach multiple times to solve the same problem. If there are multiple solutions to a problem, try to consolidate them into one. This leads to a cleaner and more maintainable codebase.

Example: In an SAP ABAP program, if multiple reports are generating similar output formats (e.g., customer invoices), rather than duplicating the code for formatting in each report, developers can create a single reusable function module. This modular approach reduces redundancy, simplifies future updates (since changes need to be made in only one place), and helps maintain consistency across the application.

KISS: Keep It Simple, Stupid

In software development, code refactoring is an ongoing journey! In my opinion, the ideal code is the kind that’s self-explanatory and easy to understand even for a junior developer. The KISS principle advocates for simplicity in design and implementation. Solutions should be as simple as possible without unnecessary complexity.

Example: Suppose you are developing a custom SAP UI5 application for tracking sales orders. Instead of implementing multiple complicated user interfaces with various navigation paths, a KISS approach would mean creating a straightforward layout with a single page for viewing and updating orders. This not only makes it easier for users to understand but also simplifies the development and maintenance processes.

 

Practical Tips for Avoiding Overengineering :

  • Conduct Regular Requirement Reviews: Maintain open lines of communication with stakeholders to ensure that requirements are clear and current. Regularly revisit these requirements to adapt to changes in the business landscape.
  • Prioritize Core Functionality: When designing solutions, focus on the features that provide the most value to users. Defer any non-essential features to future iterations.
  • Use Prototyping: Develop prototypes to test ideas before committing to full-scale implementation. This can help validate assumptions and reduce the risk of building unnecessary complexity.
  • Emphasize Documentation: Maintain clear and concise documentation of requirements, design decisions, and code. This can help prevent miscommunication and ensure that all team members are aligned on project goals.
  • Encourage Team Collaboration: Foster a culture of collaboration within your development team, encouraging open discussions about design choices and potential complexities. This collective wisdom can help identify and eliminate over-engineering tendencies.

Conclusion

Over-engineering is a common challenge in software development, but it can be avoided by adopting principles like DRY,KISS,YANGI and MVP , my Final word is here that, continued code refactoring is part of the game but always try to keep it as simple as possible.

“So if you want to go fast, if you want to get done quickly, if you want your code to be easy to write, make it easy to read.” ― Robert C. Martin, Clean Code: A Handbook of Agile Software Craftsmanship. The Clean Code Lesson 1 by Uncle Bob 😉   

 

Happy coding 🤗 😉!!

entry.title: Data locking automatically without manual intervention in SAC Planning on specific conditions
entry.content:
There is some requirement on Data locking automatically without manual intervention in SAC Planning World,

we have a Model-1 with 100 cost centers.

1.For First week of planning all 100 cost centers to be available for planing .

2.In second week Only specifc cost centers to be locked in Model-1 based on the cost centers available in Model-2
(Model-2 contains all valid cost centers) , we need to keep valid cost centers in separate model to update from Source every week.


Multi action either can lock or unlock the entire data based on parameter of dimension only not based on property of cost center dimension.

 

Summary is like Not able to lock specific cost centers- automation is required no manual intervention.

This is quite easy using driver based functionality in BPC world.

Any leads here greatly appreciated!

Thanks in Advance Surya Harsha

entry.title: A Comprehensive Guide to Using the JDBC Adapter in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
A Step-by-Step Guide to Using the JDBC Adapter in SAP Integration Suite.

The JDBC Adapter in SAP Integration Suite provides a robust mechanism for integrating SAP systems with external databases. Here's a step-by-step guide to establish a JDBC connection and perform data exchange.

Prerequisites:

Before you can effectively use the JDBC Adapter in SAP Integration Suite, ensure that the following prerequisites are met:

1. SAP Integration Suite License: You need a valid SAP Integration Suite license that includes the JDBC Adapter.

2. Database Access: You must have access to the database you want to connect to, including the necessary credentials (username, password, connection URL).

3. JDBC Driver: The appropriate JDBC driver for your database must be installed and configured in the SAP Integration Suite environment.

4. Integration Suite Environment: You need an active SAP Integration Suite environment with the necessary roles and permissions to create data sources, communication channels, and integration flows.

5. Basic Knowledge of Integration Concepts: Familiarity with integration concepts, such as message flows, adapters, and mappings, is beneficial.

6. Understanding of JDBC: A basic understanding of JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is helpful for configuring the connection and working with database-specific features.

By ensuring these prerequisites are in place, you'll be well-prepared to effectively use the JDBC Adapter in SAP Integration Suite and integrate your SAP systems with external databases.

To connect a JDBC driver in SAP Integration Suite:

  1. Navigate: Access the Integration Suite administration console.
  2. Locate JDBC Material: Go to Monitor > Integrations and APIs > JDBC Material.
  3. Add JDBC Driver: Click on the Add button to add a new JDBC driver.

Choose the JDBC driver that aligns with your specific database requirements, such as Oracle or SQL Server.

You can download the required JDBC driver from the SAP Help portal.

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/configure-jdbc-drivers

To connect a JDBC Data Source in SAP Integration Suite:

  1. Navigate: Access the Integration Suite administration console.
  2. Locate JDBC Material: Go to Monitor > Integrations and APIs > JDBC Material.
  3. Add JDBC Data Source: Click on the Add button to add a new JDBC Data Source.

IFlow:

To initiate data retrieval from a database using the JDBC adapter as a sender, you'll need to start the integration flow with a timer. The polling interval can be configured within the timer settings.

Note: The sender JDBC adapter might not be directly available in SAP Integration Suite.

Timer:

The timer purpose in the JDBC adapter within SAP Integration Suite is to control the frequency and timing of data retrieval or updates from the connected database. It allows you to schedule and automate data exchange tasks based on specific time intervals.

Scheduling: You can define the frequency at which the adapter should initiate data retrieval or update operations. This can be set in various time units, such as minutes, hours, days, or weeks.

Fetch the table data: Content modifier property details

Query:

The SELECT query should be specified in the "Query" property of the content modifier.

This ensures that the correct data is extracted and transformed based on the defined SQL statement.

Content Modifier Message Body:

${property.Query}

JDBC Adapter:

The data source name specified in the JDBC Material within the Monitoring section should be referred to as the "JDBC Data Source Alias."

This alias is used to uniquely identify and reference the data source within the integration flow configuration.

Batch Mode:

The JDBC adapter in SAP Integration Suite supports batch mode processing, which can significantly improve performance for large data volumes. Batch mode allows you to process multiple messages or data records in a single transaction, reducing the number of round trips between the SAP Integration Suite and the external database.

Limitations of Batch Mode in the JDBC Adapter in SAP Integration Suite

While batch mode in the JDBC adapter offers significant performance benefits, it's essential to be aware of its limitations:

  1. Data Volume Constraints: If you're dealing with extremely large data volumes, batch mode might still encounter performance bottlenecks. In such cases, consider alternative approaches like parallel processing or data partitioning.
  2. Transaction Rollback: If an error occurs during batch processing, the entire batch might be rolled back. This could lead to data inconsistencies or processing delays. To mitigate this, you can implement partial commit or checkpointing mechanisms within your integration flow.
  3. Complexity: Implementing batch mode can introduce additional complexity to your integration flow design. You need to carefully consider factors like batch size, interval, and error handling to ensure optimal performance and data integrity.
  4. Database Limitations: Some databases might have specific limitations or restrictions on batch processing. It's essential to consult the database documentation to understand any constraints.
  5. Performance Overhead: While batch mode can improve overall performance, there might be a slight overhead associated with managing batches and handling potential errors.

To effectively leverage batch mode and mitigate its limitations, consider the following:

  • Optimize Batch Size and Interval: Experiment with different batch size and interval settings to find the optimal configuration for your specific use case.
  • Implement Error Handling: Use appropriate error handling mechanisms to gracefully handle exceptions and prevent data loss.
  • Consider Alternative Approaches: If batch mode is not suitable for your scenario, explore other performance optimization techniques like parallel processing or data partitioning.
  • Monitor and Fine-tune: Continuously monitor the performance of your integration flow and make adjustments as needed to optimize batch processing.

By understanding these limitations and taking appropriate measures, you can effectively utilize batch mode in the JDBC adapter to enhance the performance of your SAP Integration Suite integrations.

The database response output refers to the data that is returned by the database in response to a query or request.

This output can vary widely depending on the specific query, database schema, and data stored within the database.

Following the JDBC Adapter, the integration flow can proceed to the next step based on the database response output and the defined requirements.

In many organizations, database connectivity relies on RAC Cluster databases, which typically utilize service-based connections.

RAC Cluster Database: A Comprehensive Overview

RAC (Real Application Clusters) is a high-availability, high-performance database technology developed by Oracle. It allows multiple instances of a database to run on separate nodes in a cluster, providing redundancy, scalability, and fault tolerance.

Key Features of RAC Cluster Database:

  • High Availability: RAC ensures continuous database availability by automatically failing over to a standby instance in case of a node or instance failure.
  • Scalability: It allows you to scale the database horizontally by adding more nodes to the cluster, increasing performance and capacity.
  • Load Balancing: RAC automatically distributes workload across the cluster nodes, improving performance and reducing bottlenecks.
  • Data Consistency: RAC maintains data consistency across all instances in the cluster using techniques like Fast Start Failover and Global Cache Coherency.
  • Disaster Recovery: RAC can be configured for disaster recovery by setting up a disaster recovery site with a separate RAC cluster.

Components of a RAC Cluster Database:

  • Nodes: Individual servers that run database instances.
  • Interconnect: A high-speed network that connects the nodes in the cluster.
  • Voting Disk: A shared disk that is used for voting during cluster membership changes.
  • Private Disk Groups: Shared disks that are used to store database data.
  • Cluster ware: The software that manages the cluster and ensures high availability.

Benefits of Using RAC Cluster Database:

  • Improved Performance: RAC can significantly improve database performance by distributing workload across multiple nodes.
  • Enhanced Availability: RAC provides high availability by automatically failing over to a standby instance in case of a failure.
  • Scalability: RAC allows you to scale the database horizontally to meet increasing demands.
  • Reduced Risk of Data Loss: RAC provides data redundancy and disaster recovery capabilities, minimizing the risk of data loss.
  • Simplified Management: RAC simplifies database management by automating many tasks, such as failover and load balancing.

Note: When connecting to a RAC (Real Application Clusters) database, the aforementioned process might not be successful.

Issue: com.sap.it.rt.adapter.jdbc.exceptions.JDBCException: The processing has failed due to error in creating metadatatable. : java.sql.SQLRecoverableException: IO Error: "The Network Adapter could not establish the connection"

Please refer to my other blog for a suitable solution.

Blog: Connecting to a RAC Database in SAP Integration Suite: A Step-by-Step Guide Using JDBC Adapter

Bhavani

const draftData = await SELECT.from(SystemKinds.drafts);
is giving me all properties of my entity SystemKinds plus property "HasActiveEntity" and "DraftAdministrativeData_draftUUID" of table AdminConfigServices_SystemKinds_drafts but not the properties "HasDraftEntity" and "IsActiveEntity". But this is ok and sufficiently. 
 
I was confused because of getting error messages in VSCODE around "SystemKinds.drafts" (red underlined) . If I hover over "SystemKinds.drafts", I will get typescript error, but I get no program dumps. Thus, I thought if typescript has an issue, the program will crash... 
 
But I'm still missing typescript type definition for SystemKinds.drafts in one of the .ts files in the @CDS-models folder.
 
Yours,
Stephan

entry.title: Re: error when trying to consume an orchestration with ge...
entry.content:
My bad, I was missing to set the following env variables
AICORE_CLIENT_ID: This represents the client ID.
AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: This stands for the client secret.
AICORE_AUTH_URL: This is the URL used to retrieve a token using the client ID and secret.
AICORE_BASE_URL: This is the URL of the service (with suffix /v2).
AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP: This represents the resource group that should be used.

entry.title: Re: error when trying to consume an orchestration with ge...
entry.content:
My bad, I was missing to set the following variables

  • AICORE_CLIENT_ID: This represents the client ID.
  • AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: This stands for the client secret.
  • AICORE_AUTH_URL: This is the URL used to retrieve a token using the client ID and secret.
  • AICORE_BASE_URL: This is the URL of the service (with suffix /v2).
  • AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP: This represents the resource group that should be used.
